metadata.title: DeepJoint: Robust Survival Modelling Under Clinical Presence Shift
organizations: ¹ MRC Biostatistics Unit, University of Cambridge
² Health e-Research Centre, University of Manchester

sections.title: Motivation
content: Observations'timesandtypesreflect the patient'sconditionand the clinical staff's assessment of what was needed and when.
Predictive models have eitherpartially leveragedthisclinical presenceprocess for improved performance or assumed ituninformative.
Although practitioners justifyignoringclinical presence to avoid leveraging a changeable and unpredictable process, no work has explored the impact of handling clinical presence on robustness.
Our work explores (i) how state-of-the-art methodologies behave under clinical presence shifts, and (ii) how to leverage clinical presence for improved transportability.

sections.title: Methodology
content: We studyin-ICU mortalityprediction in MIMIC III leveraginglaboratory testsafter a 24-hour observation period.
Motivated by thedifference in the counts of tests(Fig. 3) performed on patients admitted on weekends and weekdays, we hypothesise thatpracticemightdifferbetweenweekendsandweekdays.
Last- DeepSurv multi-layer perceptron relying on the last observation.
Count- Concatenate the 24-hour count of each observation to the last observation.
Ignore- An LSTM leveraging the data while ignoring its irregularity.
Resample- An LSTM with regularly resampled data.
GRU-D- Data concatenated with missingness indicators as inputs to a GRU-D.
Feature- An LSTM leveraging missingness and time elapsed since previous observation as inputs.
DeepJoint- The proposed methodology (Fig. 1) leveraging the same input as Feature.

figures.description: DeepJoint model architecture showing joint modelling of clinical presence and survival outcome
Violin plot showing difference in tests ordered stratified by admission day and outcome
Legend for admission day showing Weekday and Weekend colors
Scatter plots showing robustness performance on weekdays admissions across different horizons
Legend for models and horizons used in the robustness scatter plots

sections.title: Results
content: Figure 4 presents the transportability performance onpatients admitted on weekdaysmeasured through time-dependent C-index at three time horizons.
Insight 1- Missingness and observation times are informative of short-term outcomes.
Insight 2- Ignoring clinical presence might not only reduce performance but also hurt transportability.
Insight 3- Explicit modelling of clinical presence improves performance and robustness.

sections.title: Conclusions
content: Learning from medical data without sufficientconsiderationof the potentialinformativenessofclinical presenceonoutcomecould impair performance and transportability.
We invite practitioners to:
Explore thepractitioner-patient interactionprocess to identify clinical presence patterns.
Report theassumptionsmade on the clinical presence process.
Leverageinformative observation process.
Evaluate transportability:internallyandexternally.
